q: Sound is a sequence of pressure waves which propagates through compressible media.
Sound can propagate through media such as air or water.
During their propagation, waves cannot be reflected by the medium.
Waves can be refracted by the medium during propagation.
Sound waves can be attenuated by the medium through which they travel.
The speed of sound within a medium is independent of temperature.
A relationship between density and pressure determines the speed of sound within the medium.
The viscosity of the medium determines the rate at which sound is attenuated.
For media such as air or water, attenuation due to viscosity is significant.
Speed of a sound wave is affected by the density of the medium.
Temperature of the medium does not affect the speed of a sound wave.
The elasticity of the medium affects the speed of sound waves.
The speed of sound is the same for all different media.
In a solid medium, the speed of sound is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}}$.
In the speed formula for solids, $Y$ represents the bulk modulus.
The symbol $\rho$ in speed formulas represents the density of the material.
In liquids, the velocity of sound is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}}$.
In the speed formula for liquids, $B$ represents Young's modulus.
In gases, the velocity of sound is given by $v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P}{\rho}}$.
In the gas velocity formula, $\gamma$ is the adiabatic constant of gas.
The symbol $P$ in the gas velocity formula represents the pressure in gas.
Longitudinal sound waves travel faster in gases than they do in liquids.
Sound waves travel faster in solids than they do in liquids.
Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are elasticity properties of the medium.
The SI units of Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are $N \cdot m^2$.
The SI units of Young's modulus and the bulk modulus are $N \cdot m^{-2}$.
The level of intensity of a sound is measured relative to a reference intensity value $I_0$.
The value of $I_0$ is taken as $10^{-12} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
The reference intensity $I_0$ corresponds to the threshold of hearing.
The threshold of hearing is also known as the maximum audible intensity.
The decibel ($dB$) is the unit used for measuring sound intensity.
The intensity level of sound $\beta$ in $dB$ is defined as $10 \log_{10} \frac{I}{I_0}$.
For every $10 dB$ added to the sound level, the loudness of the sound is increased by 10 times.
A $120 dB$ sound is 10 times louder than a $100 dB$ sound.
A $120 dB$ sound is 100 times louder than a $100 dB$ sound.
Sound intensity is a direct measure of the power reaching an eardrum.
Sound intensity is measured in $W \cdot m^{-2}$.
The intensity level for a jet plane taking off is $120 dB$.
The intensity in $W \cdot m^{-2}$ for a jet plane taking off is $10$.
Loud music has an intensity level of $100 dB$.
Busy street traffic corresponds to an intensity level of $70 dB$.
The intensity of busy street traffic is $10^{-5} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
People talking have an intensity level of $50 dB$.
The intensity level of a whisper is $20 dB$.
Whisper has an intensity of $10^{-10} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
Leaves falling correspond to an intensity level of $10 dB$.
The threshold of hearing corresponds to an intensity level of $0 dB$.
The intensity of the threshold of hearing is $10^{-12} W \cdot m^{-2}$.
For every $10 dB$ added, the loudness is increased by 100 times.
Sound is a sequence of pressure waves that propagates through incompressible media.
